zh.lifeSummary: Jane Ap Rees Ap David是Mallt Ferch Ithel的女兒，於1402年出生在威爾士卡迪甘史爾。她和Sir Gruffudd ap Nicolas至少有1個女兒。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: Jane Ap Rees Ap David是Mallt Ferch Ithel的女儿，于1402年出生在威尔士卡迪甘史爾。她和Sir Gruffudd ap Nicolas至少有1个女儿。

nameMeanings.name: David
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Jewish, Welsh, Scottish, English, French, Breton, Portuguese, Czech, Croatian, and Slovenian; Hungarian (Dávid), Slovak (mainly Dávid): from the Hebrew personal name David (in Hungarian and Slovak spelled Dávid), interpreted as ‘beloved’. The name has been perennially popular among Jews, in honor of the Biblical king of this name. His prominence, and the vivid narrative of his life contained in the First Book of Samuel, led to adoption of the name among Christians in the Middle Ages in various parts of Europe. In Britain, the popularity of this as a personal name was increased for two reasons. Firstly by virtue of its being the name of the patron saint of Wales who was abbot-bishop in the 6th century at what became known as Saint David's in Pembrokeshire. There are numerous dedications and placenames honouring the saint in south Wales, and it is no coincidence that the modern surname is heavily concentrated there, especially in Glamorgan. Secondly, the name was borne by two kings of Scotland (David I, reigning 1124–53, and David II, 1329–71). Its popularity in Russia is largely due to the fact that this was the ecclesiastical name adopted by Saint Gleb (died 1015), one of two sons of Prince Vladimir of Kiev who were martyred for their Christian zeal. In North America, the English form of the surname has absorbed cognates from some other languages, especially Assyrian/Chaldean Dawid and Arabic Daud (with variants, such as Daoud and Dawood ).

History: Guillaume David from France married Marie Armand in Trois-Rivières, QC, in 1656. — This surname is listed in the (US) National Huguenot Society's register of qualified Huguenot ancestors and also in the similar register of the Huguenot Society of America.

variants.name: Davi
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Salvatore, Antonio, Guido, Rosario, Mario, Sal, Calogero, Gino, Vincenzo, Vito, Alessandra, Biagio, Biago, Carmela.
Italian (Sicily; also Davì): perhaps from an altered form of the personal name Davide (see David ).
Italian (northern, mainly Veneto): from the personal name Davio, a variant of Davino .

variants.name: Dauti
meanings: Albanian: from the Albanian Muslim personal name Daut (definite form Dauti), from Turkish Davud, an equivalent of David .

variants.name: Davey
meanings: English: from the personal name Davy, originally an Old French vernacular form of the Biblical name David which became the most common form of the name in medieval England.

variants.name: Daviau
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Armand, Aime, Aldor, Emile, Normand.
French: from a derivative of Davi, a vernacular form of the personal name David . Compare Davio and Devoid .

variants.name: Davide
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Salvatore, Gandolfo, Marco.
Italian: from the personal name Davide, an equivalent of David .

variants.name: Dawid
meanings: Polish: from the personal name Dawid, Polish form of the Biblical name David .
Assyrian/Chaldean: from the personal name Dāwīd, Syriac form of the Biblical name David . It is often spelled in the Arabized form Daud (or Daoud and Dawood ).

variants.name: Ben-David
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Jewish Shula, Ilan, Meir, Meyer, Ari, Avi, Ayal, Haim, Limor, Menashe, Mordechai, Moshe.
Jewish (from North Africa and the Ottoman Empire; also Bendavid): patronymic from the personal name David + Hebrew or vernacular Arabic ben ‘son’.

variants.name: Daud
meanings: Arabic: from the personal name Dāwūd, Arabic form of the Biblical name David . In Islam, Dāwūd is regarded as a messenger of God/Allah. Among Christians this name is found in part as an Arabized form of the Assyrian/Chaldean variant Dawid . Compare Daoud and Dawood .

variants.name: Davidenko
meanings: Ukrainian (standard transliteration Davydenko): patronymic from the personal name Davyd (see David ), formed with the diminutive suffix -enko.

variants.name: Dacus
meanings: Welsh: Anglicized form of the personal name Deicws, a pet form of Dafydd, Welsh form of David . This surname is now rare in Britain.

nameMeanings.name: Jane
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Originally a feminine form of John , from the Old French form Je(h)anne. Since the 17th century it has proved the most popular of the feminine forms of John, ahead of Joan and Jean . It now also commonly occurs as the second element in combinations such as Sarah-Jane. In Britain it is still one of the most frequent of all girls' names. It is not a royal name, but was borne by the tragic Lady Jane Grey ( 1537–54 ), who was unwillingly proclaimed queen in 1553 , deposed nine days later, and executed the following year. Seventy years earlier, the name had come into prominence as that of Jane Shore , mistress of King Edward IV and subsequently of Thomas Grey , 1st Marquess of Dorset, Lady Jane's grandfather. Jane Shore's tribulations in 1483 at the hands of Richard III , Edward's brother and successor, became the subject of popular ballads and plays, which may well have increased the currency of the name in the 16th century. A 19th-century influence was its use as the name of the central character in Charlotte Brontë's novel Jane Eyre ( 1847 ). From 1932 to 1959 it was used as the name of a cheerful and scantily clad beauty whose adventures were chronicled in a strip cartoon in the Daily Mirror. It is also borne by the American film stars Jane Russell ( 1921–2011 ) and Jane Fonda ( b. 1937 ).

variants.name: Jayne
meanings: Elaborated spelling of   Jane  , a form popularized no doubt by the American actress  Jayne Mansfield  (  Vera Jayne Palmer ,  1933–67 ) and British ice-dancer  Jayne Torvill  (  b. 1957 ).

variants.name: Jaine
meanings: Variant spelling of   Jane .

variants.name: Jain
meanings: Originally a feminine form of John , from the Old French form Je(h)anne. Since the 17th century it has proved the most popular of the feminine forms of John, ahead of Joan and Jean . It now also commonly occurs as the second element in combinations such as Sarah-Jane. In Britain it is still one of the most frequent of all girls' names. It is not a royal name, but was borne by the tragic Lady Jane Grey ( 1537–54 ), who was unwillingly proclaimed queen in 1553 , deposed nine days later, and executed the following year. Seventy years earlier, the name had come into prominence as that of Jane Shore , mistress of King Edward IV and subsequently of Thomas Grey , 1st Marquess of Dorset, Lady Jane's grandfather. Jane Shore's tribulations in 1483 at the hands of Richard III , Edward's brother and successor, became the subject of popular ballads and plays, which may well have increased the currency of the name in the 16th century. A 19th-century influence was its use as the name of the central character in Charlotte Brontë's novel Jane Eyre ( 1847 ). From 1932 to 1959 it was used as the name of a cheerful and scantily clad beauty whose adventures were chronicled in a strip cartoon in the Daily Mirror. It is also borne by the American film stars Jane Russell ( 1921–2011 ) and Jane Fonda ( b. 1937 ).

nameMeanings.name: Ap
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Short form of any of the names beginning with this syllable.

variants.name: Alexander
meanings: From the Latin form of the Greek name Alexandros, from alexein ‘to defend’ + anēr ‘man, warrior’ (genitive andros). The name became extremely popular in the post-classical period, and was borne by several individuals in the New Testament and some early Christian saints. Its use as a common given name throughout Europe, however, derives largely from the fame of Alexander the Great , King of Macedon ( 356–323 bc ), around whom a large body of popular legend grew up in late antiquity, much of which came to be embodied in the medieval ‘Alexander romances’.

variants.name: Alan
meanings: Of Celtic origin and uncertain derivation (possibly a diminutive of a word meaning ‘rock’). It was introduced into England by Breton followers of William the Conqueror , most notably Alan , Earl of Brittany, who was rewarded for his services with vast estates in the newly conquered kingdom. In Britain the variants Allan and Allen are considerably less frequent, and generally represent transferred uses of surname forms, whereas in America all three forms of the name are approximately equally common. See also Alun .

variants.name: Albert
meanings: From an Old French name, Albert, of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from adal ‘noble’ + berht ‘bright, famous’. This was adopted by the Normans and introduced by them to England, displacing the Old English form Æþelbeorht. The name is popular in a variety of forms in Western Europe, and has been traditional in a number of European princely families. It was out of favour in England for centuries, however, and the revival of its popularity in the 19th century was largely in honour of Queen Victoria's consort, Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg-Gotha .

nameMeanings.name: Rees
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Anglicized spelling of Welsh Rhys, in some cases representing a transferred use of the surname so spelled, which is derived from the Welsh given name.

variants.name: Reese
meanings: Anglicized spelling of Rhys . The name is now in widespread and frequent use outside Wales, in many cases representing a transferred use of the surname so spelled, which is derived from the given name.

variants.name: Rhys
meanings: Traditional name meaning ‘ardour’; borne in the Middle Ages by various rulers in south-west Wales, including  Rhys ap Tewdur  (  d. 1093 ) and  Rhys ap Gruffudd  (  1132–97 ).

nameMeanings.name: Ap
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Short form of any of the names beginning with this syllable.

en.lifeSummary: Jane Ap Rees Ap David was born in 1402, in Cardiganshire, Wales as the daughter of Mallt Ferch Ithel. She had at least 1 daughter with Sir Gruffudd ap Nicolas.

fr.lifeSummary: Jane Ap Rees Ap David est née en 1402, à Cardiganshire, Galles, fille de Mallt Ferch Ithel. Elle a eu au moins 1 fille avec Sir Gruffudd ap Nicolas.

de.lifeSummary: Jane Ap Rees Ap David wurde 1402 in Cardiganshire, Wales, als Tochter von Mallt Ferch Ithel geboren. Sie hatte mit Sir Gruffudd ap Nicolas mindestens 1 Tochter.

it.lifeSummary: Jane Ap Rees Ap David è nata il nel 1402 nel Cardiganshire, Galles come figlia di Mallt Ferch Ithel. Lei avuto almeno 1 figlia con Sir Gruffudd ap Nicolas.

ja.lifeSummary: Jane Ap Rees Ap Davidは1402年にウェールズカーディガンシュアイアでMallt Ferch Ithelの娘として生まれた。彼女はSir Gruffudd ap Nicolasとの間に少なくとも1人の娘をもうけた。

ko.lifeSummary: Jane Ap Rees Ap David(은)는 1402년 웨일스 카디건실르에서 Mallt Ferch Ithel의 딸로 태어났다. 그녀는 Sir Gruffudd ap Nicolas(와)과 적어도 한 명의 딸을 두었다.

pt.lifeSummary: Jane Ap Rees Ap David nasceu em 1402, em Cardiganshire, Gales filha de Mallt Ferch Ithel. Ela teve pelo menos 1 filha com Sir Gruffudd ap Nicolas.

ru.lifeSummary: Jane Ap Rees Ap David родилась в 1402 г. в местности кардиганьшир, Уэльс. Её мать звали Mallt Ferch Ithel. У неё была по крайней мере 1 дочь от Sir Gruffudd ap Nicolas.

es.lifeSummary: Jane Ap Rees Ap David nació en 1402, en Cardiganshire, Gales como hija de Mallt Ferch Ithel. Tuvo por lo menos 1 hija con Sir Gruffudd ap Nicolas.
